dprs.title: 8KB Fixed Page Size
dprs.decision: PostgreSQL uses a fixed 8KB page size (BLCKSZ) as the fundamental unit of storage and I/O.
rejected_alternatives: Variable page sizes - complexity in buffer management
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: OS cache works well with 8KB
dprs.durability_reasoning: Changing requires recompilation and full database rebuild
causal_dependencies: DPR-002: TOAST exists because of 8KB limit
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Modern NVMe SSDs use 16-32KB internal pages
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Every storage operation depends on this constant

dprs.title: TOAST - The Oversized-Attribute Storage Technique
dprs.decision: Values >2KB automatically compressed/moved to separate TOAST tables.
rejected_alternatives: Fail on large values - users need large data
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Large values accessed less frequently
dprs.durability_reasoning: Deeply integrated into tuple storage and MVCC
causal_dependencies: DPR-001: Exists because 8KB pages too small
DPR-003: MVCC must handle TOAST pointers
DPR-006: Vacuum must clean TOAST tables
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Design is stable, though modern compression could improve
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Affects query planning, storage, backup, replication

dprs.title: MVCC via Tuple Versioning in Heap
dprs.decision: MVCC by storing multiple row versions in heap with xmin/xmax for visibility.
rejected_alternatives: Single version + undo - old version access expensive
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: High-update workloads create massive bloat, long transactions block vacuum
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Every read/write depends on MVCC visibility rules

dprs.title: 32-bit Transaction ID with Wraparound
dprs.decision: XIDs are 32-bit wrapping after ~4B transactions, requiring periodic vacuum freeze.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Vacuum freeze completes in time
dprs.durability_reasoning: XID size baked into tuple header format
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: High-throughput systems hit limits quickly, large tables take hours to freeze
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Can cause database shutdown and data loss

dprs.title: HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) Updates
dprs.decision: UPDATEs not changing indexed columns and fitting on same page create HOT chains without index updates.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Most updates don't change indexed columns
INFERRED: Chain traversal < index update cost
dprs.durability_reasoning: Critical performance optimization for update-heavy workloads
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Many indexed columns or large rows may not benefit
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Affects update performance, index maintenance, vacuum

dprs.title: Vacuum as Separate Maintenance Process
dprs.decision: Dead tuple cleanup via separate VACUUM process, not inline.
rejected_alternatives: Undo log auto-cleanup - undo storage complexity
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: TB+ tables take hours, high update rates outpace vacuum, cloud I/O costs
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Affects bloat, performance, wraparound safety, storage costs

dprs.title: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) for Durability
dprs.decision: All modifications written to sequential WAL before data pages.
rejected_alternatives: Synchronous data writes - random I/O too slow
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Sequential WAL >> random data writes
dprs.durability_reasoning: Foundation of durability and crash recovery
causal_dependencies: DPR-013: Full-page writes for torn pages
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: NVMe blurs sequential vs random I/O, cloud storage different guarantees
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Every write generates WAL, enables replication/backup/recovery

dprs.title: Autovacuum as Background Daemon
dprs.decision: Background daemon auto-schedules VACUUM/ANALYZE based on table activity.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Cost throttling prevents I/O overwhelm
dprs.durability_reasoning: Critical for preventing bloat and wraparound
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: 20% of 1TB = 200GB too much, high-velocity overwhelms workers
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Misconfiguration is top cause of production issues

dprs.title: Multi-Process Architecture
rejected_alternatives: Event loop - can't use multiple cores
causal_dependencies: DPR-011: Shared memory for buffer pool
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: High connection counts consume excessive memory, cloud charges for memory

dprs.title: Snapshot Isolation as Default
dprs.decision: READ COMMITTED and REPEATABLE READ snapshot isolation via MVCC.
explicit_constraints: READ COMMITTED: new snapshot per statement
REPEATABLE READ: snapshot at transaction start
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Apps use explicit locking when needed

dprs.title: Shared Buffer Pool with Clock-Sweep
dprs.decision: Shared buffer pool with clock-sweep (LRU approximation) eviction.
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Large working sets may not fit, cloud RAM expensive, NVMe reduces cache miss cost
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Every read/write goes through buffer pool

dprs.title: Checkpoint-Based Crash Recovery
dprs.decision: Periodic checkpoints write all dirty buffers, allowing recovery from last checkpoint.
rejected_alternatives: Continuous writing - recovery replays all WAL
explicit_constraints: Triggered by time (5min) or WAL volume
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Checkpoint I/O completes in time
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Large shared_buffers make checkpoints I/O intensive, cloud IOPS limits
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Affects recovery time, I/O patterns, WAL volume

dprs.title: Full-Page Writes for Torn Page Protection
dprs.decision: First modification after checkpoint writes entire 8KB page to WAL.
rejected_alternatives: Checksums only - detect but don't fix
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: Torn pages rare but catastrophic
dprs.decay_risk_reasoning: Storage still doesn't guarantee atomic 8KB writes
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Significantly increases WAL volume (2-3x after checkpoints)

dprs.title: Streaming Replication via WAL Shipping
dprs.decision: Replication by streaming WAL from primary to standby for replay.
implicit_assumptions: INFERRED: WAL contains all needed info

dprs.title: External Connection Pooling Required
dprs.decision: Connection pooling handled externally (pgBouncer, pgPool) rather than in-server.
explicit_constraints: External tool required for high connection counts
dprs.blast_radius_reasoning: Required for production deployments with many connections

ctg_edges.explanation: 8KB page size forces TOAST for large values
HOT updates must fit within 8KB page
Checkpoints write dirty buffers from pool
Vacuum assumes manageable dead tuple rate

assumptions.assumption: Vacuum can keep up with tuple version creation rate
assumptions.decay_evidence: High-update workloads on cloud storage creating bloat faster than vacuum can clean
assumptions.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT schemaname, tablename, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup, round(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 2) as dead_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_dead_tup > 10000 ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC

assumptions.assumption: Databases won't exceed 2B transactions between vacuum freeze operations
assumptions.decay_evidence: High-throughput OLTP systems (100K+ TPS) can generate 2B transactions in ~6 hours
assumptions.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) as xid_age, 2000000000 - age(datfrozenxid) as xids_until_wraparound FROM pg_database WHERE datallowconn ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC

assumptions.assumption: Tables are small enough to vacuum in reasonable time
assumptions.decay_evidence: TB-scale tables taking 6-12 hours to vacuum, cloud storage IOPS limits making vacuum expensive
assumptions.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT pid, datname, relid::regclass, phase, heap_blks_total, heap_blks_scanned, round(100.0 * heap_blks_scanned / NULLIF(heap_blks_total, 0), 2) as pct_complete FROM pg_stat_progress_vacuum

assumptions.assumption: Default thresholds (20% of table) are appropriate for most workloads
assumptions.decay_evidence: 20% of 1TB table = 200GB of changes before vacuum triggers, causing massive bloat
assumptions.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT schemaname, tablename, n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del as total_changes, n_live_tup, round(100.0 * (n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del) / NULLIF(n_live_tup, 0), 2) as change_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE pg_total_relation_size(schemaname||'.'||tablename) > 10737418240 ORDER BY total_changes DESC

assumptions.assumption: shared_buffers should be 25-40% of system RAM
assumptions.decay_evidence: Cloud instances with limited RAM make large buffer pools expensive, NVMe reducing cache miss penalty
assumptions.recommended_monitor_query: SELECT sum(heap_blks_read) as heap_read, sum(heap_blks_hit) as heap_hit, round(100.0 * sum(heap_blks_hit) / NULLIF(sum(heap_blks_hit) + sum(heap_blks_read), 0), 2) as cache_hit_ratio FROM pg_statio_user_tables

counterfactual_traces.question: What if PostgreSQL used 16KB pages instead of 8KB?
counterfactual_traces.original_decision: PostgreSQL uses a fixed 8KB page size (BLCKSZ) as the fundamental unit of storage and I/O.
counterfactual_traces.reasoning: Modern NVMe SSDs use 16-32KB internal pages. 16KB would reduce TOAST usage and improve I/O efficiency.

counterfactual_traces.target_title: MVCC via Tuple Versioning in Heap
counterfactual_traces.question: What if PostgreSQL used undo logs instead of in-heap tuple versioning?
counterfactual_traces.original_decision: MVCC by storing multiple row versions in heap with xmin/xmax for visibility.
counterfactual_traces.alternative: Use undo tablespace for old row versions
counterfactual_traces.reasoning: Undo-based MVCC eliminates table bloat, removes need for aggressive vacuum, prevents XID wraparound.

impacts.title: 32-bit Transaction ID with Wraparound
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use undo tablespace for old row versions...' propagates via 'requires' to 32-bit Transaction ID with Wraparound.

impacts.title: HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) Updates
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use undo tablespace for old row versions...' propagates via 'enables' to HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) Updates.

impacts.title: Vacuum as Separate Maintenance Process
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use undo tablespace for old row versions...' propagates via 'requires' to Vacuum as Separate Maintenance Process.

impacts.title: Autovacuum as Background Daemon
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use undo tablespace for old row versions...' propagates via 'requires' to Autovacuum as Background Daemon.

impacts.title: Snapshot Isolation as Default
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use undo tablespace for old row versions...' propagates via 'enables' to Snapshot Isolation as Default.

counterfactual_traces.question: What if PostgreSQL used 64-bit transaction IDs?
counterfactual_traces.original_decision: XIDs are 32-bit wrapping after ~4B transactions, requiring periodic vacuum freeze.
counterfactual_traces.reasoning: 64-bit XIDs never wrap around, eliminating vacuum freeze and forced shutdown risk.

impacts.title: MVCC via Tuple Versioning in Heap
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use 64-bit XIDs eliminating wraparound...' propagates via 'assumption_of' to MVCC via Tuple Versioning in Heap.

impacts.title: HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) Updates
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use 64-bit XIDs eliminating wraparound...' propagates via 'assumption_of' to HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) Updates.

impacts.title: Vacuum as Separate Maintenance Process
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use 64-bit XIDs eliminating wraparound...' propagates via 'requires' to Vacuum as Separate Maintenance Process.

impacts.title: Autovacuum as Background Daemon
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use 64-bit XIDs eliminating wraparound...' propagates via 'requires' to Autovacuum as Background Daemon.

impacts.title: Snapshot Isolation as Default
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Use 64-bit XIDs eliminating wraparound...' propagates via 'assumption_of' to Snapshot Isolation as Default.

counterfactual_traces.question: What if PostgreSQL adopted multi-threaded architecture?
counterfactual_traces.reasoning: Threading reduces memory per connection, eliminates need for external poolers like pgBouncer.

impacts.title: Shared Buffer Pool with Clock-Sweep
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Thread-per-connection with shared address space...' propagates via 'requires' to Shared Buffer Pool with Clock-Sweep.

impacts.title: Checkpoint-Based Crash Recovery
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Thread-per-connection with shared address space...' propagates via 'requires' to Checkpoint-Based Crash Recovery.

impacts.would_change: Change to 'Thread-per-connection with shared address space...' propagates via 'requires' to Full-Page Writes for Torn Page Protection.

impacts.title: External Connection Pooling Required
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Thread-per-connection with shared address space...' propagates via 'requires' to External Connection Pooling Required.

counterfactual_traces.question: What if PostgreSQL used shadow paging instead of WAL?
counterfactual_traces.original_decision: All modifications written to sequential WAL before data pages.
counterfactual_traces.alternative: Copy-on-write shadow paging for crash recovery
counterfactual_traces.reasoning: Shadow paging eliminates WAL writes but complicates streaming replication.

impacts.title: Checkpoint-Based Crash Recovery
impacts.would_change: Change to 'Copy-on-write shadow paging for crash recovery...' propagates via 'enables' to Checkpoint-Based Crash Recovery.

impacts.would_change: Change to 'Copy-on-write shadow paging for crash recovery...' propagates via 'requires' to Full-Page Writes for Torn Page Protection.

impacts.would_change: Change to 'Copy-on-write shadow paging for crash recovery...' propagates via 'enables' to Streaming Replication via WAL Shipping.

most_impactful_dprs.title: MVCC via Tuple Versioning in Heap
